content: What is the percent void volume in hcp lattice?
explanation:
Packing efficiency in hcp crystal lattice $=74 \%$.

$\therefore$ Percentage of void volume in hcp crystal lattice $=100-74=26 \%$

content: Calculate the number of unit cells present in 0.6 g metal if the product of density and unit cell volume is $3.6 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{~g}$.
explanation: $$ \begin{aligned} & \text { Number of unit cells in } x \mathrm{~g} \text { of metal }=\frac{x}{\rho \mathrm{a}^3} \\ & =\frac{0.6}{3.6 \times 10^{-22}}=1.666 \times 10^{21} \end{aligned} $$

content: Find the total number of tetrahedral voids formed in 0.5 mole of a compound forming hcp structure.
explanation:
Given:

Compound forms hcp structure and we have 0.5 mole of it.

Step 1: Relation between atoms and tetrahedral voids

For any close-packed structure (fcc or hcp):

  • Number of tetrahedral voids = 2 × number of atoms.

Step 2: Number of atoms in 0.5 mole

1 mole contains $ N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} $ atoms.

So, $ 0.5\ \text{mole} = 0.5 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.011 \times 10^{23} $ atoms.

Step 3: Number of tetrahedral voids

$ \text{Number of tetrahedral voids} = 2 \times \text{number of atoms} $

$ = 2 \times 3.011 \times 10^{23} $

$ = 6.022 \times 10^{23} $

✅ Final Answer:

$ \boxed{6.022 \times 10^{23}} $

Correct Option: C

content: Which of the following statements is NOT true about vacancy defect?

options.content: It arises when a particle is missing from its regular site in the crystal lattice during crystallisation.
The vacancy defect can also be developed when the substance is heated.
In this the mass of substance decreases.
The density of crystal remains same when vacancy defect is created.

explanation:
Option A:

"It arises when a particle is missing from its regular site in the crystal lattice during crystallisation."

✅ True.

This is the definition of a vacancy defect.

Option B:

"The vacancy defect can also be developed when the substance is heated."

✅ True.

At higher temperatures, atoms gain enough energy to leave their lattice sites, so heating increases vacancy defects.

Option C:

"In this the mass of substance decreases."

✅ True.

When atoms or ions leave their regular lattice sites, the total mass of the crystal decreases slightly.

Option D:

"The density of crystal remains same when vacancy defect is created."

❌ Not True.

When atoms are missing, the mass decreases but the volume remains nearly the same, so the density decreases.

✅ Correct answer: Option D

Explanation: The density of the crystal decreases when a vacancy defect is created.

content: Calculate the volume occupied by a particle in simple cubic unit cell if the total volume of unit cell is $4.0 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~cm}^3$.
explanation:
Total volume occupied by particles in simple unit cell

$$ \begin{aligned} & =\frac{\pi \mathrm{a}^3}{6}=0.5237 \mathrm{a}^3 \\ & =0.5237 \times 4.0 \times 10^{-23} \quad\left(\because \mathrm{~V}=\mathrm{a}^3\right) \\ & =2.0948 \times 10^{-23} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$

content: In ionic solid, anions are arranged in ccp array and cations occupy all the octahedral voids. What is the formula of ionic compound? [consider $\mathrm{A}=$ cation; $\mathrm{B}=$ anion]
explanation:
No. of particles in ccp unit cell / structure $=4$

$\therefore \quad$ No. of anions $=4$

Octahedral voids are equal to the number of particles i.e., 4 per unit cell

cations (A) occupy all octahedral voids.

$\therefore \quad$ No. of cations $=4$

$\therefore \quad$ Formula of compound can be given as $=\mathrm{A}_4 \mathrm{~B}_4=\mathrm{AB}$

content: Calculate the volume of bcc unit cell if the radius of an atom in it is 216.5 pm .
explanation:
Step 1: Relation between edge length $(a)$ and atomic radius $(r)$ in bcc

For a body-centered cubic (bcc) unit cell:

$$ a = \frac{4r}{\sqrt{3}} $$

Step 2: Substitute $r = 216.5$ pm into the formula

$$ a = \frac{4 \times 216.5~\text{pm}}{\sqrt{3}} $$

Calculate numerator:

$$ 4 \times 216.5 = 866~\text{pm} $$

Calculate denominator:

$$ \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732 $$

So

$$ a = \frac{866}{1.732}~\text{pm} \approx 500~\text{pm} $$

Step 3: Find the volume of the unit cell in $\text{pm}^3$

$$ \text{Volume} = a^3 = (500~\text{pm})^3 = 125 \times 10^6~\text{pm}^3 $$

Step 4: Convert $\text{pm}^3$ to $\text{cm}^3$

Recall:

$1~\text{pm} = 10^{-12}~\text{m} = 10^{-10}~\text{cm}$

So,

$$ 1~\text{pm}^3 = (10^{-10})^3~\text{cm}^3 = 10^{-30}~\text{cm}^3 $$

So,

$$ \text{Volume} = 125 \times 10^6 \times 10^{-30}~\text{cm}^3 = 1.25 \times 10^8 \times 10^{-30}~\text{cm}^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-22}~\text{cm}^3 $$

Final Answer:

Option B

$$ 1.25 \times 10^{-22}~\text{cm}^3 $$

content:
Calculate the number of atoms in 0.4 gram of metal if it forms fcc structure.

$$ \left[\rho \times \mathrm{a}^3=1.2 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{~g}\right] $$
explanation:
Given:

  • Mass of metal sample = $0.4$ g

  • $[\rho \times a^3 = 1.2 \times 10^{-22}\ \text{g}]$

  • Structure is fcc.

Step 1: Number of atoms per unit cell ($Z$) for fcc

For fcc (face-centered cubic) structure:

$$ Z = 4 $$

Step 2: Mass of one unit cell

Given:

$$ \rho \times a^3 = 1.2 \times 10^{-22}\ \text{g} $$

This value directly gives the mass of one unit cell.

So, mass of 1 unit cell $= 1.2 \times 10^{-22}\ \text{g}$.

Step 3: Number of unit cells in $0.4$ g

Number of unit cells $= \dfrac{\text{Total mass}}{\text{Mass of 1 unit cell}}$

$$ \text{Number of unit cells} = \frac{0.4}{1.2 \times 10^{-22}} = \frac{0.4}{1.2} \times 10^{22} = 0.333\ldots \times 10^{22} = 3.33 \times 10^{21} $$

Step 4: Total number of atoms

Each unit cell (fcc) has 4 atoms, so:

$$ \text{Total number of atoms} = \text{Number of unit cells} \times Z = 3.33 \times 10^{21} \times 4 = 1.332 \times 10^{22} $$

Final Answer:

$ \boxed{1.333 \times 10^{22}} $

So, the correct option is Option B.

content: Calculate the molar mass of an element having density $19.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ if it forms fcc structure $\left[\mathrm{a}^3 \times \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}=40 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right]$
explanation:
Step 1: In an fcc unit cell, n = 4 atoms.

Step 2: Given values are ρ = 19.2 g cm⁻³ and a³ × Nₐ = 40 cm³ mol⁻¹.

Step 3: Use the relation between density and molar mass:

$$ \begin{aligned} \rho &=\frac{M \times n}{N_A(a)^3} \\ M &=\frac{\rho \times N_A \times(a)^3}{n} \\ &=\frac{40 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}{4} \times 19.2 \mathrm{gcm}^{-3}=192 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \end{aligned} $$

Step 4: Therefore, the molar mass M = 192 g mol⁻¹.

content: Calculate the volume occupied by particle in simple cubic unit cell if volume of unit cell is $5.5 \times 10^{-22} \mathrm{~cm}^3$.
explanation:
Step 1: Calculate the cell edge length:
$$ a^3 = 5.5 \times 10^{-22}\,\mathrm{cm}^3 $$

Step 2: Volume occupied by particle $=4 \pi r^3$. For SCC, $r = a/2$.

Step 3: Substitute and simplify:
$$ \begin{aligned} &= \frac{4}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times \left(\frac{a}{2}\right)^3 = \frac{5.5 \times 10^{-22} \times 11}{7 \times 3} \\ &= 2.88 \times 10^{-22}\,\mathrm{cm}^3 \end{aligned} $$

content: What is the minimum number of spheres required to develop an octahedral void?
explanation:
In close‐packed arrangements (like CCP or HCP), an octahedral void is the space at the centre of an octahedron formed by touching spheres. By definition:

An octahedron has 6 vertices.

Placing one sphere at each vertex of that octahedron creates the void at its centre.

Therefore, the minimum number of spheres required is 6.

Answer: Option C (6)
